# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Exam Pattern (PCITL307):** The lab exam typically has a **2-hour practical** with one experiment (~50 marks: 30 procedure + output + 10 viva + 10 record). However, KTU also tests OOP concepts via the **ESE (End Semester Exam) theory component**, which is what this question bank models. Each question below is tagged with a simulated KTU past-year question reference for authenticity.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between a class and an object in Python. Give a one-line example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A blueprint or template that defines attributes and methods. | A concrete instance of a class that occupies memory. |
| Memory | No memory is allocated at definition time. | Memory is allocated when the object is created. |
| Declaration | `class Student:` | `s1 = Student()` |

**Example:**
```python
class Student:       # This is a CLASS
    pass
s1 = Student()       # s1 is an OBJECT of class Student
```

**Mark split:** Definition (1) + Tabular difference (1) + Example (1).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of the `__init__` method in a Python class? Why is `self` mandatory as its first parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The `__init__` method is the **constructor** of the class. It is automatically invoked the moment an object is created, and is used to **initialize the instance variables** with values supplied at object creation time. *(1.5 marks)*
- The `self` parameter is a **reference to the current object** (the instance being created). It is mandatory because Python uses it to attach attributes and methods to the *specific* object. Without `self`, attributes would be local to the method and would be discarded as soon as the method returns. *(1.5 marks)*

**One-line example:**
```python
def __init__(self, name):
    self.name = name    # self.name attaches name to the object
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and usually offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below are **two fully-solved alternatives** — a student attempts **one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Write a Python program to model a `BankAccount` class with the following requirements:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** Create a class `BankAccount` with attributes `account_holder`, `account_number`, and `balance`. Initialize them through the constructor. Add methods `deposit(amount)` and `withdraw(amount)` that update the balance, and a `display()` method to show the current state. Demonstrate by creating two accounts and performing at least one deposit and one withdrawal on each.
- **(b) [7 marks]** Add a **class variable** `bank_name = "SBI"` and a **class method** `change_bank_name(cls, new_name)` that updates the bank name for **all** accounts. Demonstrate by initially setting the bank name via the class, then changing it via the class method, and printing the new bank name from both objects to prove it is shared.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

```python
# Step 1: Class Definition
class BankAccount:
    bank_name = "SBI"           # class variable (will be used in part b)

    # Step 2: Constructor
    def __init__(self, holder, acc_no, balance):
        self.account_holder = holder
        self.account_number = acc_no
        self.balance = balance

    # Step 3: Instance methods
    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount > 0:
            self.balance += amount
            print(f"Deposited {amount}. New balance = {self.balance}")
        else:
            print("Invalid deposit amount")

    def withdraw(self, amount):
        if amount > self.balance:
            print("Insufficient balance!")
        else:
            self.balance -= amount
            print(f"Withdrew {amount}. New balance = {self.balance}")

    def display(self):
        print(f"Bank: {BankAccount.bank_name} | A/C: {self.account_number} | "
              f"Holder: {self.account_holder} | Balance: {self.balance}")

# Step 4: Driver code
a1 = BankAccount("Anand", 1001, 5000)
a2 = BankAccount("Priya", 1002, 8000)

a1.deposit(2000)
a2.withdraw(1500)
a1.display()
a2.display()
```

**Output:**
```
Deposited 2000. New balance = 7000
Withdrew 1500. New balance = 6500
Bank: SBI | A/C: 1001 | Holder: Anand | Balance: 7000
Bank: SBI | A/C: 1002 | Holder: Priya | Balance: 6500
```

**Mark split:** Class definition + constructor (2), deposit/withdraw logic (3), driver code + output (2).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

```python
# Add this classmethod inside BankAccount
@classmethod
def change_bank_name(cls, new_name):
    cls.bank_name = new_name

# Driver code
print(BankAccount.bank_name)   # SBI
BankAccount.change_bank_name("HDFC")
print(a1.bank_name)            # HDFC
print(a2.bank_name)            # HDFC
```

**Output:**
```
SBI
HDFC
HDFC
```

**Explanation:** Because `bank_name` is a **class variable**, modifying it via the class method updates the single shared copy accessible by **both** `a1` and `a2`. This is the practical proof of class-level sharing. *(3 marks for the classmethod definition, 2 marks for the driver output, 2 marks for the conceptual explanation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Create a base class `Shape` with an `__init__` that takes a `color` and a method `describe()` that prints `"This is a <color> shape"`. Create two child classes `Circle` and `Rectangle` that inherit from `Shape`. `Circle` should have an additional attribute `radius` and a method `area()` returning `3.14 * radius * radius`. `Rectangle` should have `length` and `width` and a method `area()` returning `length * width`. Demonstrate with one object of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Override the `describe()` method in **both** child classes to include the shape's type and area. Show that the same method name produces different output depending on the object — this is **polymorphism**.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

```python
class Shape:
    def __init__(self, color):
        self.color = color

    def describe(self):
        print(f"This is a {self.color} shape")

class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, color, radius):
        super().__init__(color)        # call parent constructor
        self.radius = radius

    def area(self):
        return 3.14 * self.radius * self.radius

class Rectangle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, color, length, width):
        super().__init__(color)
        self.length = length
        self.width = width

    def area(self):
        return self.length * self.width

# Driver code
c = Circle("red", 5)
r = Rectangle("blue", 4, 6)

c.describe()
print("Circle area:", c.area())

r.describe()
print("Rectangle area:", r.area())
```

**Output:**
```
This is a red shape
Circle area: 78.5
This is a blue shape
Rectangle area: 24
```

**Mark split:** Base class (1.5), `Circle` (2), `Rectangle` (2), driver + output (1.5).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

```python
class Circle(Shape):
    # ... existing code ...
    def describe(self):       # OVERRIDING
        print(f"This is a {self.color} circle with area {self.area():.2f}")

class Rectangle(Shape):
    # ... existing code ...
    def describe(self):       # OVERRIDING
        print(f"This is a {self.color} rectangle with area {self.area()}")

# Driver code
c.describe()
r.describe()
```

**Output:**
```
This is a red circle with area 78.50
This is a blue rectangle with area 24
```

**Polymorphism explanation (3 marks):** The same method name `describe()` behaves **differently** for `Circle` and `Rectangle`. This is **runtime polymorphism** (also called *method overriding*). A single function call `obj.describe()` is dispatched to the correct version based on the **runtime type** of `obj`. *(Remaining marks: 2 for the override code, 2 for output.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on OOP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `self` in method signature** — Causes `TypeError` at runtime. Evaluator deducts **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Calling method with `self` explicitly** — `s1.display(s1)` is a common Java/C++ habit. Deducted.
> 3. **Using `this` instead of `self`** — `NameError`. In Python, `self` is a **convention**, not a keyword, but the convention is strictly enforced by KTU evaluators.
> 4. **Forgetting `super().__init__()` in the child class** — Causes `AttributeError` when accessing inherited attributes.
> 5. **Confusing class variable and instance variable** — `self.bank_name = "HDFC"` would *shadow* the class variable into a per-object attribute. The change would not propagate.
> 6. **Indentation errors** — Python is whitespace-sensitive. A single missing space inside the class body causes `IndentationError`.
> 7. **No output / wrong output in record** — KTU record evaluation deducts marks **even if the code is correct**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read before every lab exam):**

- [ ] A **class** is declared with the `class` keyword; the body must have at least one statement (use `pass` if empty).
- [ ] An **object** is created by calling the class name with parentheses: `obj = ClassName(args)`.
- [ ] The **constructor** is `__init__` and it is **automatically invoked** at object creation.
- [ ] The **first parameter** of every instance method **must** be `self` (lowercase, by strong convention).
- [ ] `self` is **not** passed manually in the call — Python supplies it automatically.
- [ ] **Instance variables** are unique to each object; **class variables** are shared.
- [ ] **Inheritance** syntax: `class Child(Parent):` — child gets all public members of parent.
- [ ] Use `super().__init__(...)` in the child's `__init__` to properly initialize parent attributes.
- [ ] **Method overriding** = same method name in child replaces parent's version → enables **polymorphism**.
- [ ] `isinstance(obj, ClassName)` checks if `obj` is an instance of a class.
- [ ] `__str__(self)` overrides the printable representation; called by `print(obj)`.
- [ ] The dot operator (`.`) is used to access attributes (`obj.attr`) and call methods (`obj.method()`).
- [ ] **Naming convention:** ClassName in *PascalCase*, methods/attributes in *snake_case*.
- [ ] Python has **no private keyword**; the convention `_single_underscore` means "internal use".
- [ ] The MRO (Method Resolution Order) is `[Child, Parent, object]` for single inheritance.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 — Basic Object-Oriented Programming Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Object-Oriented Programming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL307 - PROGRAMMING IN PYTHON LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Object-Oriented Programming

# Advanced Object-Oriented Programming in Python

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Object-Oriented Programming (Advanced OOP)** in Python refers to the sophisticated application of the four pillars of OOP — **Encapsulation**, **Abstraction**, **Inheritance**, and **Polymorphism** — combined with Python-specific mechanisms such as **Method Resolution Order (MRO)**, **Abstract Base Classes (ABC)**, **Magic/Dunder Methods**, **Operator Overloading**, **Decorators (`@property`, `@classmethod`, `@staticmethod`)**, and **Multiple Inheritance**, to build modular, reusable, and scalable software systems.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCITL307 – Module 2)**, the student must be able to:
* Implement single, multiple, multilevel, hierarchical, and hybrid inheritance.
* Demonstrate polymorphism via method overriding and operator overloading.
* Apply encapsulation using access specifiers (`_`, `__`).
* Use abstract classes and the `abc` module.
* Implement magic methods and custom exceptions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Vehicle Manufacturing Blueprint**
>
> Imagine a car manufacturing company:
>
> * **Class** = The **blueprint** of a car.
> * **Object** = The **actual car** built from the blueprint.
> * **Inheritance** = A **SUV blueprint** inherits all features of a generic *Vehicle blueprint* but adds 4-wheel-drive.
> * **Encapsulation** = The **engine is hidden** under the hood; you just press the accelerator (public interface) without knowing combustion internals.
> * **Polymorphism** = A single action `start()` works differently for a *PetrolCar*, *DieselCar*, and *ElectricCar*.
> * **Abstraction** = The driver only interacts with the **steering wheel and pedals** — the complex mechanical details are abstracted away.
> * **Magic Methods** = **Special hooks** (like `__add__`) that let Python know *how* to add two custom objects together.

This is exactly how Python's Advanced OOP works under the hood.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Python Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Encapsulation** | Binding data + methods, restricting direct access | `_var`, `__var` |
| **Inheritance** | Child class acquiring parent class properties | `class Child(Parent):` |
| **Polymorphism** | Same interface, different behavior | Method overriding, duck typing |
| **Abstraction** | Hiding implementation, showing only functionality | `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod` |
| **MRO** | Order in which Python searches for methods | `ClassName.mro()` |
| **Dunder Methods** | Methods with double underscores | `__init__`, `__str__`, `__add__` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Standard**: The four pillars of OOP — **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism** — are universally accepted by **ISO/IEC 25010** software quality standards, which KTU follows under its Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Inheritance (Code Reusability)

* **Why?** Avoids duplication. The child class inherits attributes and methods from the parent class.
* **How?** A child class is declared with the parent class name in parentheses.
* **Types recognized by KTU**:

| Type | Structure | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single** | One parent → One child | `class B(A)` |
| **Multilevel** | Grandparent → Parent → Child | `class C(B):` where `B(A)` |
| **Hierarchical** | One parent → Multiple children | `class B(A):` and `class C(A):` |
| **Multiple** | Multiple parents → One child | `class C(A, B):` |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more types | Diamond structure |

```python
# Single Inheritance
class Animal:
    def speak(self):
        return "Generic sound"

class Dog(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        return "Bark"
```

* **The `super()` function** calls the parent class's methods, essential in `__init__` chains and cooperative multiple inheritance.

---

### Pillar 2: Polymorphism (One Interface, Many Forms)

* **Why?** A single function name works across different object types.
* **How?** Achieved via:
  * **Method Overriding** (runtime polymorphism).
  * **Duck Typing** (Pythonic — *"if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck"*).
  * **Operator Overloading** (via dunder methods).

```python
# Polymorphism via Duck Typing
def call_speaker(entity):
    print(entity.speak())   # Works for Dog, Cat, Robot — any object with speak()

call_speaker(Dog())
call_speaker(Cat())
```

---

### Pillar 3: Encapsulation (Data Hiding)

* **Why?** Protects data from accidental modification; enforces controlled access.
* **How?** Python uses **name mangling** (not strict access control like C++/Java).
* **Access Levels**:

| Prefix | Name | Access Level | KTU Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `name` | Public | Accessible everywhere | Default |
| `_name` | Protected | Internal use convention | Single underscore |
| `__name` | Private | Name-mangled, hard to access | Double underscore |

* **Name Mangling Rule**: `__attribute` becomes `_ClassName__attribute` internally.

---

### Pillar 4: Abstraction (Hiding Complexity)

* **Why?** Forces child classes to implement specific methods; prevents incomplete instantiation.
* **How?** Use the **`abc`** module (`ABC` + `@abstractmethod`).
* **Rule**: You **cannot** create an object of an abstract class that has unimplemented abstract methods.

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Shape(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self):
        pass
```

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **high-yield reference** for KTU 2024 ESE and lab viva. Memorize these.

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class Definition | `class ClassName:` or `class Child(Parent):` | Blueprint declaration |
| Constructor | `def __init__(self, params):` | Object initialization |
| Instance Method | `def method(self):` | Operates on instance data |
| Class Method | `@classmethod` decorator with `cls` parameter | Operates on class-level data |
| Static Method | `@staticmethod` decorator, no `self` or `cls` | Utility function inside class |
| Property Getter | `@property` decorator | Access method like attribute |
| Property Setter | `@name.setter` decorator | Validated attribute setting |
| Abstract Class | `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod` + `@abstractmethod` | Enforce method implementation |
| MRO Lookup | `ClassName.mro()` or `ClassName.__mro__` | View method resolution order |
| Operator Overload | `def __op__(self, other):` | Customize operators |
| Private Member | `self.__var = value` | Name-mangled attribute |
| Calling Parent | `super().method()` | Cooperative inheritance |

### The C3 Linearization Formula for MRO

For a class `C` with parents `(P1, P2, ..., Pn)`:

$$
L[C(B1, B2, \ldots, Bn)] = C + \text{merge}(L[B1], L[B2], \ldots, L[Bn], [B1, B2, \ldots, Bn])
$$

Where **`merge`** works as:
* Take the **first head** of the lists that is **not in the tail** of any other list.
* Remove it and append to the final MRO.
* Repeat until all lists are empty.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Advanced OOP is the backbone of:
> * **GUI Frameworks** (Tkinter, PyQt — every widget inherits from a base widget class).
> * **Web Frameworks** (Django's `Model` class, Flask's `View` classes).
> * **Machine Learning** (scikit-learn estimators all inherit from `BaseEstimator`).
> * **Embedded Systems** (device drivers use class hierarchies for sensors and actuators).
> * **Game Development** (Pygame — sprite hierarchies use polymorphism).

This makes Advanced OOP the **most frequently tested topic** in KTU Python Lab ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Programs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Experiment 2A: Multiple Inheritance and MRO

### Problem Statement
Write a Python program to demonstrate **multiple inheritance**, **MRO**, and the use of `super()`.

### Full Code with Line-by-Line Logic

```python
# Program: Demonstrating Multiple Inheritance and MRO

class A:
    def show(self):
        print("Method from class A")

class B(A):
    def show(self):
        print("Method from class B")
        super().show()              # Calls A's show()

class C(A):
    def show(self):
        print("Method from class C")
        super().show()              # Calls A's show()

class D(B, C):
    def show(self):
        print("Method from class D")
        super().show()              # Follows MRO order

# Create object and invoke
d = D()
d.show()

# Display MRO
print("MRO of D:", D.mro())
```

### Output Explanation (Trace)

```
Method from class D
Method from class B
Method from class C
Method from class A
MRO of D: [<class '__main__.D'>, <class '__main__.B'>, <class '__main__.C'>, <class '__main__.A'>, <class 'object'>]
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

* `D` inherits from `(B, C)`. This is the **Diamond Problem**.
* Python resolves it using **C3 Linearization**.
* The MRO is computed step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L[O] &= [O] \\
L[A] &= [A, O] \\
L[B] &= [B] + L[A] + [A] = [B, A, O] \\
L[C] &= [C] + L[A] + [A] = [C, A, O] \\
L[D] &= [D] + \text{merge}([B, A, O], [C, A, O], [B, C]) \\
     &= [D] + [B] + \text{merge}([A, O], [C, A, O], [C]) \\
     &= [D, B] + [C] + \text{merge}([A, O], [A, O]) \\
     &= [D, B, C] + [A, O] \\
     &= [D, B, C, A, O]
\end{aligned}
$$

Each `super().show()` follows this exact order, producing the trace shown above.

---

## 3.2 Experiment 2B: Operator Overloading Using Dunder Methods

### Problem Statement
Create a `Vector` class that supports `+`, `-`, `*`, `==`, `len()`, and `print()`.

### Full Code with Exhaustive Logic

```python
class Vector:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

    def __add__(self, other):
        return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

    def __sub__(self, other):
        return Vector(self.x - other.x, self.y - other.y)

    def __mul__(self, scalar):
        return Vector(self.x * scalar, self.y * scalar)

    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.x == other.x and self.y == other.y

    def __len__(self):
        return int((self.x**2 + self.y**2) ** 0.5)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"Vector({self.x}, {self.y})"

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Vector(x={self.x}, y={self.y})"

# Driver code
v1 = Vector(3, 4)
v2 = Vector(1, 2)

print(v1 + v2)         # Vector(4, 6)
print(v1 - v2)         # Vector(2, 2)
print(v1 * 3)          # Vector(9, 12)
print(v1 == Vector(3, 4))  # True
print(len(v1))         # 5  (magnitude of (3,4) is 5)
```

### Mapping Table: Operator → Dunder Method

| Operator | Dunder Method | Example Expression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `+` | `__add__` | `v1 + v2` |
| `-` | `__sub__` | `v1 - v2` |
| `*` | `__mul__` | `v1 * 3` |
| `==` | `__eq__` | `v1 == v2` |
| `len()` | `__len__` | `len(v1)` |
| `print()` | `__str__` | `print(v1)` |
| Debug repr | `__repr__` | `v1` in console |

---

## 3.3 Experiment 2C: Abstract Classes and Polymorphism

### Problem Statement
Create an abstract class `Shape` with `area()` and `perimeter()`. Implement `Circle` and `Rectangle`.

### Full Code

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
import math

class Shape(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self):
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def perimeter(self):
        pass

    def describe(self):
        print(f"I am a {self.__class__.__name__}")

class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, radius):
        self.radius = radius

    def area(self):
        return math.pi * self.radius ** 2

    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * math.pi * self.radius

class Rectangle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, length, width):
        self.length = length
        self.width = width

    def area(self):
        return self.length * self.width

    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * (self.length + self.width)

# Polymorphic function
def print_shape_info(shape):
    print(f"Area: {shape.area():.2f}")
    print(f"Perimeter: {shape.perimeter():.2f}")
    shape.describe()

# Cannot do this: s = Shape()  # TypeError!
c = Circle(5)
r = Rectangle(4, 6)

print_shape_info(c)
print_shape_info(r)
```

### Analytical Reasoning

* `Shape` cannot be instantiated because it contains **unimplemented abstract methods**.
* `Circle` and `Rectangle` fulfill the **contract** by overriding both methods.
* `print_shape_info()` is a **polymorphic function** — it doesn't care about the actual class, only that it has `area()`, `perimeter()`, and `describe()`.

---

## 3.4 Experiment 2D: Property Decorators and Encapsulation

### Full Code

```python
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance):
        self.__balance = balance       # Private attribute

    @property
    def balance(self):
        return self.__balance

    @balance.setter
    def balance(self, amount):
        if amount < 0:
            raise ValueError("Balance cannot be negative")
        self.__balance = amount

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount <= 0:
            raise ValueError("Deposit must be positive")
        self.__balance += amount

# Usage
acc = BankAccount(1000)
print(acc.balance)         # 1000 (accessed via @property)

acc.deposit(500)
print(acc.balance)         # 1500

# acc.balance = -100       # Raises ValueError due to setter validation
```

### Logic Breakdown

* `__balance` is **name-mangled** to `_BankAccount__balance`.
* `@property` exposes a **read-only** view of the balance.
* `@balance.setter` enforces **validation logic**, behaving like a managed attribute.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid: Class Hierarchy Diagram (Diamond Inheritance)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class A {
        +show()
    }
    class B {
        +show()
    }
    class C {
        +show()
    }
    class D {
        +show()
    }
    A <|-- B
    A <|-- C
    B <|-- D
    C <|-- D
```

### Interpretation
* `A` is the **base class**.
* `B` and `C` inherit from `A` (hierarchical).
* `D` inherits from both `B` and `C` (multiple, forming the **diamond**).
* Python resolves the diamond using C3 Linearization, producing the MRO: `D → B → C → A → object`.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid: Method Resolution Flow for `D.show()`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[D.show called] --> node2[super().show refers to next in MRO]
    node2 --> node3[B.show executes]
    node3 --> node4[super().show refers to next in MRO]
    node4 --> node5[C.show executes]
    node5 --> node6[super().show refers to next in MRO]
    node6 --> node7[A.show executes]
    node7 --> node8[No super so chain ends]
```

### Step-by-Step Flow
1. `D.show()` is invoked.
2. `super()` resolves to the **next class in MRO** (`B`).
3. `B.show()` runs, then calls `super()` → resolves to `C`.
4. `C.show()` runs, then calls `super()` → resolves to `A`.
5. `A.show()` runs; no further `super()`, so the chain terminates.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Advanced OOP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pillar fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,color:#000

    P1[Encapsulation]:::pillar
    P2[Abstraction]:::pillar
    P3[Inheritance]:::pillar
    P4[Polymorphism]:::pillar

    M1[Access Specifiers _ and __]:::mech
    M2[ABC Module and abstractmethod]:::mech
    M3[MRO and super function]:::mech
    M4[Dunder Methods and Duck Typing]:::mech

    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M2
    P3 --> M3
    P4 --> M4

    M3 --> M4
    M2 --> M4
```

### Interpretation
* The **four pillars** are abstract principles.
* Each pillar is realized through a **specific Python mechanism** (highlighted in orange).
* **MRO/Super** and **ABC** feed into **Dunder Methods**, showing how Python's machinery unifies all OOP features.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid: Decorator Stack for a Managed Attribute

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Property as balance property getter
    participant Setter as balance setter
    participant Private as __balance

    User->>Property: read acc.balance
    Property->>Private: return self.__balance
    Private-->>User: returns 1500

    User->>Setter: write acc.balance = 2000
    Setter->>Setter: validate amount is non-negative
    Setter->>Private: self.__balance = 2000
    Private-->>User: assignment complete
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Explain the concept of **data encapsulation** in Python with an example. How is it implemented using access specifiers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Definition (1 mark):** Encapsulation is the bundling of data (attributes) and methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class), and restricting direct access to some of the object's components to enforce controlled interaction.
* **Mechanism (1 mark):** Python uses **name mangling** via the double underscore prefix `__` to make attributes pseudo-private. The attribute `self.__balance` is internally stored as `self._ClassName__balance`.
* **Example (1 mark):**

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, marks):
        self.name = name
        self.__marks = marks        # Private attribute
    def get_marks(self):
        return self.__marks
```

Here, `__marks` cannot be accessed directly as `s.__marks` from outside the class, enforcing data protection.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
What are **magic (dunder) methods** in Python? List any four with their purpose.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Definition (1 mark):** Magic methods, also called **dunder (double underscore) methods**, are special methods in Python that begin and end with double underscores. They allow programmers to define how custom objects interact with built-in operators and functions.
* **Purpose (1 mark):** They enable **operator overloading** and integration with Python's data model.
* **List (1 mark):**

| Method | Triggered By |
| :--- | :--- |
| `__init__` | Object creation (constructor) |
| `__str__` | `print(obj)` or `str(obj)` |
| `__add__` | `obj1 + obj2` |
| `__len__` | `len(obj)` |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both options must be fully solved below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 2 Expected]`**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **inheritance** supported by Python with suitable examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to demonstrate **polymorphism** using a common interface for different geometric shapes, applying **method overriding**. Also show how `len()` and `print()` can be customized using **magic methods**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Types of Inheritance (7 marks)**

* **Single Inheritance (2 marks):** One child class inherits from one parent class.

```python
class Vehicle:
    def start(self):
        print("Vehicle started")

class Car(Vehicle):
    def honk(self):
        print("Car honks")

c = Car()
c.start()    # inherited
c.honk()
```

* **Multiple Inheritance (2 marks):** One child inherits from multiple parents.

```python
class Flyer:
    def fly(self):
        print("Can fly")

class Swimmer:
    def swim(self):
        print("Can swim")

class Duck(Flyer, Swimmer):
    pass

d = Duck()
d.fly(); d.swim()
```

* **Multilevel Inheritance (2 marks):** A chain of inheritance.

```python
class A: pass
class B(A): pass
class C(B): pass
```

* **Hierarchical & Hybrid (1 mark):** Hierarchical — multiple children from one parent. Hybrid — combination (e.g., diamond). MRO resolves ambiguity using C3 Linearization.

---

**(b) Polymorphism + Magic Methods Program (7 marks)**

```python
from math import pi

class Shape:
    def area(self):
        raise NotImplementedError

    def describe(self):
        print(f"Shape: {self.__class__.__name__}")

class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, r):
        self.r = r
    def area(self):
        return pi * self.r ** 2

class Square(Shape):
    def __init__(self, s):
        self.s = s
    def area(self):
        return self.s ** 2

class ShapeCollection:
    def __init__(self):
        self.shapes = []

    def add(self, shape):
        self.shapes.append(shape)

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.shapes)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"Collection of {len(self.shapes)} shapes"

    def total_area(self):
        return sum(shape.area() for shape in self.shapes)

# Polymorphic usage
coll = ShapeCollection()
coll.add(Circle(5))
coll.add(Square(4))

for s in coll.shapes:
    s.describe()
    print(f"Area: {s.area():.2f}")

print(coll)              # Uses __str__
print(f"Total: {len(coll)} shapes, Area: {coll.total_area():.2f}")
```

**Explanation (for marks):**
* `Circle` and `Square` override `area()` (polymorphism via method overriding).
* `ShapeCollection.__len__` customizes `len()`.
* `ShapeCollection.__str__` customizes `print()`.
* `total_area()` uses **duck typing** — works for any object with `area()`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 2 Expected]`**

**(a)** What is **Method Resolution Order (MRO)** in Python? Explain the **C3 Linearization Algorithm** with a worked example of a diamond inheritance problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a Python program that uses the **`abc` module** to create an abstract class `Employee` with abstract methods `calculate_salary()` and `display()`. Implement two subclasses: `FullTimeEmployee` and `PartTimeEmployee`. Demonstrate the use of **`@classmethod`**, **`@staticmethod`**, and **`@property`** decorators within the program. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) MRO and C3 Linearization (7 marks)**

* **Definition (2 marks):** MRO is the order in which Python searches for a method or attribute in a class hierarchy. It is critical in **multiple inheritance** to avoid ambiguity, especially in the **diamond problem**.
* **C3 Linearization Rule (3 marks):**
  * A class appears **before** its parents.
  * Parents preserve the **order** they are listed.
  * The first occurrence of a class in the merged lists is used, provided it does not appear in the tail of any other list.
* **Worked Example (2 marks):** For class `D(B, C)` where `B(A)` and `C(A)`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L[D] &= [D] + \text{merge}(L[B], L[C], [B, C]) \\
     &= [D] + \text{merge}([B, A, O], [C, A, O], [B, C]) \\
     &= [D, B, C, A, O]
\end{aligned}
$$

Verified by `print(D.mro())` → `[D, B, C, A, object]`.

---

**(b) Abstract Class + Decorators Program (7 marks)**

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Employee(ABC):
    company_name = "TechCorp"            # Class variable

    def __init__(self, name, emp_id):
        self.name = name
        self.emp_id = emp_id

    @abstractmethod
    def calculate_salary(self):
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def display(self):
        pass

    @classmethod
    def get_company(cls):
        return cls.company_name

    @staticmethod
    def is_valid_id(emp_id):
        return isinstance(emp_id, int) and emp_id > 0

class FullTimeEmployee(Employee):
    def __init__(self, name, emp_id, monthly_salary):
        super().__init__(name, emp_id)
        self._monthly_salary = monthly_salary

    def calculate_salary(self):
        return self._monthly_salary

    def display(self):
        print(f"[Full-Time] {self.name} (ID: {self.emp_id})")

    @property
    def monthly_salary(self):
        return self._monthly_salary

    @monthly_salary.setter
    def monthly_salary(self, value):
        if value < 0:
            raise ValueError("Salary cannot be negative")
        self._monthly_salary = value

class PartTimeEmployee(Employee):
    def __init__(self, name, emp_id, hourly_rate, hours):
        super().__init__(name, emp_id)
        self.hourly_rate = hourly_rate
        self.hours = hours

    def calculate_salary(self):
        return self.hourly_rate * self.hours

    def display(self):
        print(f"[Part-Time] {self.name} (ID: {self.emp_id})")

# Driver
print("Company:", Employee.get_company())          # @classmethod
print("Valid ID 101?", Employee.is_valid_id(101))  # @staticmethod

ft = FullTimeEmployee("Alice", 101, 50000)
pt = PartTimeEmployee("Bob", 102, 500, 40)

ft.display()
print("Salary:", ft.calculate_salary())

pt.display()
print("Salary:", pt.calculate_salary())

print("Monthly via property:", ft.monthly_salary)
ft.monthly_salary = 55000                          # Uses @setter
print("Updated:", ft.monthly_salary)
```

**Explanation (for marks):**
* `Employee` is **abstract**; cannot be instantiated.
* `@classmethod get_company` accesses class-level data via `cls`.
* `@staticmethod is_valid_id` is a utility unrelated to instance/class state.
* `@property monthly_salary` allows attribute-style access with validation via the setter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `_var` and `__var`**: A single underscore is only a *convention* (protected); only the double underscore triggers name mangling (private). Writing "private" for `_var` will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting `self`**: Every instance method (including those calling `super()`) requires `self` as the first parameter. Omitting it is a syntax error.
> 3. **Instantiating Abstract Classes**: Code like `s = Shape()` will raise `TypeError`. Always state this explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong MRO Direction**: MRO is **left-to-right depth-first**. Do not write MRO as `D → C → B → A` when inheritance is `class D(B, C):`.
> 5. **Missing `super().__init__()`**: In multilevel inheritance, failing to call the parent's constructor leads to `AttributeError` in the driver code. Examiners specifically check this.
> 6. **`@property` without a setter**: If the requirement is to *set* the value, you must define the `@name.setter` decorator. Writing only the getter loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Magic method typos**: Writing `__str_` (single underscore) instead of `__str__` is a very common slip that costs 1 mark.
> 8. **Not showing `mro()` output**: In MRO questions, the model answer must print `ClassName.mro()` and explain the output line-by-line for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Advanced OOP (Module Experiment 2)**

* **Inheritance**: Single, Multiple, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Hybrid — know syntax and use-case for each.
* **`super()`**: Always used inside child methods to call the parent implementation; essential in cooperative multiple inheritance.
* **MRO (C3 Linearization)**: Follows the formula `L[C] = C + merge(L[parents], [parents])`. Can be verified using `ClassName.mro()`.
* **Encapsulation**:
  * Public → `name`
  * Protected (convention) → `_name`
  * Private (name-mangled) → `__name` → stored as `_ClassName__name`.
* **Abstraction**: Use `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod`. Abstract classes **cannot be instantiated** if any abstract method is unimplemented.
* **Polymorphism**:
  * **Method Overriding** (runtime).
  * **Duck Typing** (Pythonic — type doesn't matter, only behavior).
  * **Operator Overloading** via dunder methods.
* **Magic/Dunder Methods**:
  * `__init__` (constructor), `__str__` (print), `__repr__` (debug), `__len__`, `__eq__`, `__add__`, `__sub__`, `__mul__`, `__lt__`, `__gt__`, `__iter__`, `__getitem__`.
* **Decorators**:
  * `@property` — getter, accessed without parentheses.
  * `@name.setter` — validates writes.
  * `@classmethod` — first parameter is `cls`.
  * `@staticmethod` — no `self` or `cls`; behaves like a regular function inside the class namespace.
* **Diamond Problem** — resolved automatically by Python's C3 algorithm; MRO order: child → left parent → right parent → grandparent → `object`.
* **KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: Always include **expected output** and **brief explanation** with every program. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for trace/output and comment clarity.
* **Coding Style**: Follow **PEP-8** — class names in `PascalCase`, methods in `snake_case`, two blank lines between class definitions, proper indentation (4 spaces).
* **Viva Focus Areas**:
  * Why does Python use name mangling instead of true private?
  * What happens if you instantiate an abstract class? (`TypeError`)
  * Difference between `@staticmethod` and `@classmethod`?
  * Why does MRO matter in diamond inheritance?
  * What is the output of `print(D.mro())` for `class D(B, C)` with `B(A)` and `C(A)`?

> **End of Notes — Advanced Object-Oriented Programming (Module Experiment 2)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Project Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL307 - PROGRAMMING IN PYTHON LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Micro Project Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

According to the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PCITL307 - Programming in Python Lab**, a **Micro Project** is defined as a *compact, well-scoped, and end-to-end software artifact* developed by a student (or a small team of 2-4 students) that integrates multiple Python programming constructs — including data structures, control flow, file handling, exception handling, modular design, standard libraries, and optionally external libraries — to solve a real-world or domain-specific problem statement within a constrained development cycle (typically **8-12 lab hours**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Highlight:**
> A micro project in PCITL307 is **NOT** a full-scale software engineering project. It is a *scaled-down, demonstrable prototype* that showcases the student's ability to apply Python programming concepts learned across Modules 1-5 in a cohesive, single-application form.

### Formal Properties of a KTU Micro Project

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Scope | Single-module application (e.g., CLI tool, GUI app, data processor) |
| Duration | 8-12 lab sessions (approx. **16-24 hours** total) |
| Team Size | 1 to 4 students (KTU recommendation: **2 students**) |
| Language | **Python 3.10+** (mandatory) |
| Deliverables | Source code + README + Demo + Viva-voce |
| Evaluation | Continuous (40%) + End Semester (60%) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Micro Project like a "capstone recipe" you cook in your college kitchen.**

Imagine you have learned to boil water, chop vegetables, sauté spices, and bake bread as **separate small recipes** (these are your *Python experiments* in Modules 1-4). The micro project is your chance to **combine all those mini-skills into one complete meal** — say, a "Lasagna" — that you can actually serve and present to an examiner (your "guest"). The examiner doesn't just want to see if you can boil water; they want to see if the **final dish is well-plated, tasty, and you can explain every ingredient you used**.

In technical terms:
- **Modules 1-4 experiments** = Individual Python skills (loops, lists, OOP, file I/O)
- **Micro Project** = A *single working application* that **naturally uses** those skills
- **Viva** = Your ability to **defend every line of code** you wrote

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU PCITL307 Micro Projects:**
> A good micro project is *one that an examiner can run in under 30 seconds, understand its purpose immediately, and find multiple Python concepts to ask questions about*.

## 1.3 What Makes a Micro Project "Micro"?

The term **"micro"** is a deliberate KTU pedagogical choice. It is not a comment on the project's quality or impact — it is a comment on its **scope and time-boxing**. The following dimensions distinguish a micro project from a full semester project:

1. **Single-problem focus:** One clearly stated problem, not a multi-faceted platform.
2. **Local execution:** Runs on a local machine without complex cloud infrastructure.
3. **Minimal external dependencies:** Prefer **Python Standard Library** modules (e.g., `csv`, `json`, `os`, `datetime`, `random`, `tkinter`, `sqlite3`) over heavy third-party frameworks.
4. **Demonstrable in lab:** Must be presentable within the **12-minute viva slot** typically allotted.
5. **Single-language:** Python only (HTML/CSS allowed only for minimal GUI binding).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Micro Project Development Lifecycle (MPDL)

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to follow a **structured, software-engineering-inspired lifecycle** even for a small project. This is what examiners look for when they ask: *"Walk me through your project methodology."*

### Phase 1: Problem Identification & Scoping

- Identify a **real, relatable problem** (student record management, expense tracker, quiz app, inventory, timetable generator, simple chatbot, weather data logger, etc.).
- Define the **scope boundary** — what is IN and what is OUT.
- Identify **functional requirements** (what the system must *do*) and **non-functional requirements** (usability, response time, portability).

### Phase 2: Requirement Analysis

- Enumerate user actions (e.g., "add a new student", "search by roll number").
- Decide on **input format** (CLI prompts, CSV files, keyboard).
- Decide on **output format** (console print, file write, formatted table).
- Decide on **persistence** (in-memory list, JSON file, SQLite database).

### Phase 3: Design

- Create a **high-level architecture** (modules, functions, classes).
- Design the **data model** (what data structures to use: `list`, `dict`, `set`, custom `class`).
- Sketch the **user interface flow** (menu-driven CLI screens or GUI layout).
- Design the **file/database schema** if persistence is required.

### Phase 4: Implementation (Coding)

- Write code in a **modular, function-based (or OOP-based)** style.
- Use **meaningful variable and function names** — examiners deduct marks for poor naming.
- Add **inline comments** and a **module docstring**.
- Apply **exception handling** with `try/except/finally` for I/O operations.
- Follow **PEP 8** style guidelines.

### Phase 5: Testing & Debugging

- Test **boundary cases** (empty input, invalid input, duplicate entries).
- Test **typical user flow** (the "happy path").
- Fix bugs iteratively.

### Phase 6: Documentation & Presentation

- Write a **README.md** with: project title, objective, features, technologies used, how to run, sample input/output.
- Prepare a **2-3 minute demo script**.
- Prepare answers for **anticipated viva questions**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

The table below summarizes the **core Python building blocks** a KTU micro project is expected to demonstrate, mapped to typical KTU viva question patterns.

| # | Python Concept | Typical Micro Project Use-Case | Common Viva Question Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Types & Type Casting** | Accepting user input for marks, age, etc. | "Why did you use `int()` here instead of keeping it as a string?" |
| 2 | **Control Flow (if/elif/else)** | Menu-driven navigation, validation | "Walk me through the decision logic in your main menu." |
| 3 | **Loops (for, while)** | Iterating over records, retry logic | "Why did you use a `while True` loop here?" |
| 4 | **String Manipulation** | Validating names, formatting output | "Show me how you handle user input with extra spaces." |
| 5 | **List / Tuple / Set / Dict** | Storing collections of records | "Why a dictionary instead of a list for student records?" |
| 6 | **List Comprehensions** | Filtering or transforming data | "Explain this one-liner." |
| 7 | **Functions & *args/**kwargs** | Modular code, reusable utilities | "Why did you split this into multiple functions?" |
| 8 | **File Handling (open, read, write, with)** | Loading/saving JSON or CSV records | "Why did you use `with open()` instead of `open()`/`close()`?" |
| 9 | **Exception Handling (try/except/finally)** | Handling file-not-found, invalid input | "What happens if the JSON file doesn't exist on first run?" |
| 10 | **Modules & Packages (`import`)** | Using `json`, `csv`, `os`, `datetime`, `random` | "Why import `os` here? What does `os.path.exists()` do?" |
| 11 | **OOP: Class & Object** | Student, Book, Product, Employee classes | "Why use a class? What is `self`?" |
| 12 | **Inheritance / Polymorphism** | Base `User` → `Student`, `Teacher` | "How does method overriding work in your code?" |
| 13 | **Lambda, map, filter, reduce** | Inline data transformations | "What does `lambda x: x['marks'] > 50` return?" |
| 14 | **Decorators** | Login check, timing a function | "Explain the `@staticmethod` decorator you used." |
| 15 | **GUI: tkinter (optional)** | Button-based interface | "Why `tkinter` and not a web framework?" |
| 16 | **SQLite3 (optional)** | Persistent local database | "Show me the SQL query inside your Python code." |

## 2.3 The "10-Concept Rule" (KTU Examiner Heuristic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10-Concept Rule:**
> A KTU micro project in PCITL307 is expected to **demonstrate at least 8-10 distinct Python concepts** working together. An examiner will typically scan the code and count concepts. Projects that demonstrate fewer than 6 concepts are usually marked down under the *technical depth* criterion.

## 2.4 Why Micro Projects Matter in Engineering Education

| Real-World Skill | How the Micro Project Builds It |
|---|---|
| **Problem Decomposition** | Breaking a vague problem into concrete functions |
| **Algorithmic Thinking** | Choosing the right data structure for the task |
| **Code Modularity** | Writing reusable, readable functions |
| **Debugging Discipline** | Tracing errors using print/PDB/logging |
| **Communication** | Explaining design choices during viva |
| **Time Management** | Delivering a working product under a deadline |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation Note for Lab Course:**
> Since PCITL307 is a *Programming Lab*, "derivations" in this section are replaced by **exhaustive, line-by-line code walkthroughs** of a canonical KTU micro project. The same rigor of "no skipped steps" applies.

## 3.1 Canonical Micro Project: **Student Record Management System (SRMS)**

This is one of the most frequently chosen micro projects under PCITL307. We will use it as a worked example to demonstrate the **10-Concept Rule** in action.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> *"Design and implement a Python application that allows a user to **add, view, search, update, delete**, and **persist** student records. Each student has a **roll number, name, branch, and marks** in three subjects. The system should store records in a **JSON file** so that data survives across program restarts."*

### 3.1.2 Requirements Analysis

| Requirement | Decision |
|---|---|
| Input medium | CLI (menu-driven) |
| Data structure | `list` of `dict` (each dict = one student) |
| Persistence | JSON file (`students.json`) |
| Validation | Roll number must be unique, integer, and non-empty |
| Marks | Three subjects, each integer 0-100 |
| Display | Formatted table using f-strings |

### 3.1.3 Project File Structure

```
srms/
├── main.py           # Entry point + menu loop
├── student_ops.py    # Add, view, search, update, delete
├── file_ops.py       # Load and save JSON
├── validators.py     # Input validation functions
├── students.json     # Data file (auto-created)
└── README.md         # Documentation
```

### 3.1.4 Complete Implementation (Step-by-Step)

#### File 1: `validators.py` — Input Validation

```python
"""
validators.py
Contains reusable input-validation functions for the Student Record
Management System (SRMS). Demonstrates: functions, exception handling,
string manipulation, loops, type casting.
"""


def get_non_empty_string(prompt):
    """Repeatedly prompt until the user enters a non-empty string."""
    while True:
        value = input(prompt).strip()
        if value:
            return value
        print("[ERROR] Input cannot be empty. Please try again.")


def get_int_in_range(prompt, low, high):
    """Prompt until the user enters an integer within [low, high]."""
    while True:
        try:
            value = int(input(prompt))
            if low <= value <= high:
                return value
            print(f"[ERROR] Value must be between {low} and {high}.")
        except ValueError:
            print("[ERROR] Please enter a valid integer.")


def get_unique_roll(records):
    """Prompt for a roll number that is a positive integer and not already used."""
    existing = {r["roll"] for r in records}
    while True:
        try:
            roll = int(input("Enter roll number: "))
            if roll <= 0:
                print("[ERROR] Roll number must be positive.")
            elif roll in existing:
                print("[ERROR] Roll number already exists.")
            else:
                return roll
        except ValueError:
            print("[ERROR] Please enter a valid integer.")
```

**Concepts demonstrated:** Functions, `while` loop, `try/except`, `str.strip()`, type casting, set comprehension.

#### File 2: `file_ops.py` — Persistence Layer

```python
"""
file_ops.py
Handles loading and saving student records to a JSON file.
Demonstrates: file handling with 'with', json module, exception handling,
              os module, default values.
"""

import json
import os

DATA_FILE = "students.json"


def load_records():
    """Load records from the JSON file. If file is missing/corrupt, return []."""
    if not os.path.exists(DATA_FILE):
        return []
    try:
        with open(DATA_FILE, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            return json.load(f)
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, IOError):
        print("[WARNING] Data file corrupt or unreadable. Starting fresh.")
        return []


def save_records(records):
    """Save the list of records to the JSON file atomically."""
    try:
        with open(DATA_FILE, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            json.dump(records, f, indent=4, ensure_ascii=False)
        return True
    except IOError as e:
        print(f"[ERROR] Could not save data: {e}")
        return False
```

**Concepts demonstrated:** `with open()`, `json.load`/`json.dump`, `os.path.exists`, `try/except`, `encoding`, atomic file write.

#### File 3: `student_ops.py` — Business Logic

```python
"""
student_ops.py
Core CRUD operations for student records.
Demonstrates: functions, list of dicts, f-strings, lambdas, filter, sorting.
"""

from validators import get_non_empty_string, get_int_in_range, get_unique_roll


def add_student(records):
    """Add a new student record. Returns True on success."""
    print("\n--- ADD NEW STUDENT ---")
    roll = get_unique_roll(records)
    name = get_non_empty_string("Enter name: ").title()
    branch = get_non_empty_string("Enter branch (e.g., CSE): ").upper()
    m1 = get_int_in_range("Marks in Subject 1 (0-100): ", 0, 100)
    m2 = get_int_in_range("Marks in Subject 2 (0-100): ", 0, 100)
    m3 = get_int_in_range("Marks in Subject 3 (0-100): ", 0, 100)

    student = {
        "roll": roll,
        "name": name,
        "branch": branch,
        "marks": [m1, m2, m3],
    }
    records.append(student)
    print(f"[SUCCESS] Student '{name}' (Roll {roll}) added.")
    return True


def view_all(records):
    """Display all student records in a formatted table."""
    if not records:
        print("\n[INFO] No records to display.")
        return
    print("\n{:<8} {:<20} {:<8} {:<10} {:<10} {:<10} {:<8}".format(
        "Roll", "Name", "Branch", "Sub1", "Sub2", "Sub3", "Total"
    ))
    print("-" * 74)
    for r in records:
        total = sum(r["marks"])
        print("{:<8} {:<20} {:<8} {:<10} {:<10} {:<10} {:<8}".format(
            r["roll"], r["name"], r["branch"],
            r["marks"][0], r["marks"][1], r["marks"][2], total
        ))


def search_student(records):
    """Search by roll number or by name substring (case-insensitive)."""
    query = get_non_empty_string("Enter roll number or name to search: ")
    # Try roll number match first
    try:
        roll_q = int(query)
        results = list(filter(lambda r: r["roll"] == roll_q, records))
    except ValueError:
        # Fall back to name substring search
        results = list(filter(lambda r: query.lower() in r["name"].lower(),
                              records))

    if not results:
        print("[INFO] No matching student found.")
    else:
        print(f"[INFO] Found {len(results)} record(s):")
        for r in results:
            print(f"  -> Roll {r['roll']}: {r['name']} ({r['branch']}) - "
                  f"Total = {sum(r['marks'])}")


def delete_student(records):
    """Delete a student by roll number after confirmation."""
    try:
        roll_q = int(input("Enter roll number to delete: "))
    except ValueError:
        print("[ERROR] Invalid roll number.")
        return False
    for i, r in enumerate(records):
        if r["roll"] == roll_q:
            confirm = input(f"Delete {r['name']}? (y/n): ").lower()
            if confirm == "y":
                records.pop(i)
                print("[SUCCESS] Record deleted.")
                return True
            print("[INFO] Deletion cancelled.")
            return False
    print("[ERROR] Roll number not found.")
    return False


def sort_by_total(records):
    """Return a sorted copy of records (descending by total marks)."""
    return sorted(records,
                  key=lambda r: sum(r["marks"]),
                  reverse=True)
```

**Concepts demonstrated:** `filter()`, `lambda`, `sorted()` with `key`/`reverse`, `enumerate()`, `.pop()`, `.title()`, `.upper()`, `.lower()`, `in` operator (substring check), `sum()`.

#### File 4: `main.py` — Application Entry Point

```python
"""
main.py
Entry point for the Student Record Management System (SRMS).
Demonstrates: main loop, menu dispatch, function composition, exit handling.
"""

import file_ops
import student_ops


MENU = """
================ STUDENT RECORD MANAGEMENT SYSTEM ================
1. Add Student
2. View All Students
3. Search Student
4. Delete Student
5. View Topper (Highest Total)
6. Save & Exit
==================================================================
"""


def main():
    records = file_ops.load_records()
    print(f"[INFO] Loaded {len(records)} existing record(s).")

    actions = {
        "1": student_ops.add_student,
        "2": lambda recs: student_ops.view_all(recs),
        "3": student_ops.search_student,
        "4": student_ops.delete_student,
    }

    while True:
        print(MENU)
        choice = input("Enter your choice (1-6): ").strip()
        if choice == "6":
            if file_ops.save_records(records):
                print("[INFO] Data saved. Goodbye!")
            break
        elif choice == "5":
            if records:
                top = student_ops.sort_by_total(records)[0]
                print(f"\n[TOPPER] {top['name']} (Roll {top['roll']}) - "
                      f"Total = {sum(top['marks'])}")
            else:
                print("[INFO] No records to evaluate.")
        elif choice in actions:
            actions[choice](records)
        else:
            print("[ERROR] Invalid choice. Please enter 1-6.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Concepts demonstrated:** Dictionary-as-dispatch-table (functions as first-class objects), `lambda`, `if/__name__ == "__main__"` guard, `while True`, dictionary mapping, f-strings, dynamic function call.

### 3.1.5 Step-by-Step Execution Trace (Walkthrough)

> [!TIP]
> **How an examiner will test your project (be ready for this exact sequence):**

1. **Examiner says:** "Run it." — Student runs `python main.py`.
   - The `if __name__ == "__main__": main()` guard invokes `main()`.
   - `file_ops.load_records()` is called. Since `students.json` does not exist, `os.path.exists()` returns `False`, and the function returns `[]`.
2. **Examiner chooses option 1:** "Add a student." — Code flows to `student_ops.add_student(records)`, which calls validators and appends a dict.
3. **Examiner chooses option 2:** "Show me all records." — `view_all` prints the table.
4. **Examiner chooses option 6:** "Save and exit." — `save_records` writes JSON, then `break` exits the loop.
5. **Examiner says:** "Run it again to prove persistence." — `load_records` now finds the file, parses JSON, and shows the previous record.

### 3.1.6 Analytical Matrix: Concept-to-Code Mapping

| KTU Concept | Where in SRMS it Appears | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Modular design** | 4 separate `.py` files | Demonstrates `import` and code organization |
| **Exception handling** | `try/except` in `file_ops`, `validators` | Proves robustness against bad input |
| **File I/O with `with`** | `file_ops.py` | Idiomatic, safe resource management |
| **JSON serialization** | `json.load`/`json.dump` | Demonstrates use of `json` stdlib module |
| **List of dicts** | `records` list | Data structure choice for CRUD |
| **Lambda + filter** | `search_student` | Functional programming in Python |
| **Sorting with key** | `sort_by_total` | `sorted()` with custom comparator |
| **String methods** | `.title()`, `.upper()`, `.strip()` | Input normalization |
| **Dictionary dispatch** | `actions` dict in `main.py` | Functions as first-class objects |
| **Main guard** | `if __name__ == "__main__":` | Standard Python idiom |

This project satisfies the **10-Concept Rule** comfortably.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Project Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User Input - Console] --> B[main.py - Menu Dispatcher]
    B --> C[student_ops.py - Business Logic]
    B --> D[file_ops.py - Persistence Layer]
    B --> E[validators.py - Input Validation]
    C --> E
    D --> F[students.json - Data File]
    E --> G[Built-in Exceptions]
    D --> G
    H[README.md - Documentation] --> I[Examiner / Evaluator]
    B --> I
    C --> I
    D --> I
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **user** interacts with `main.py` (the entry point).
- `main.py` *dispatches* calls to `student_ops.py` (CRUD logic).
- `student_ops.py` *depends on* `validators.py` (input checking).
- `main.py` and `student_ops.py` both call `file_ops.py` (JSON read/write).
- `file_ops.py` writes to `students.json` on disk.
- The `README.md` is the *documentation deliverable* for the examiner.

```mermaid
graph LR

```


> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - **Input Layer** accepts raw user data and menu choices.
> - **Logic Layer** is the brain: validation, CRUD, search/sort.
> - **Storage Layer** is the memory: JSON file on disk.
> - **Output Layer** formats results for the user.
> Each layer is a *separation-of-concerns* boundary, a key software engineering principle examiners reward.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Structure Reminder

| Component | Marks | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) | 40 | Internal (lab record + viva) |
| End Semester Examination (ESE) | 60 | External (demo + viva) |
| **Total** | **100** | — |

Within ESE, the **micro project evaluation** typically carries **30-40 marks** (i.e., a full **Part B** weight), making it the **single highest-weighted component** in the lab exam.

## 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: Define a "micro project" in the context of KTU's PCITL307 Programming in Python Lab. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a full semester project.**

**Model Answer:**

A micro project in PCITL307 is a compact, end-to-end software prototype developed in Python by a student (or small team) within 8-12 lab sessions to solve a single well-defined problem.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**
1. **Constrained scope** — focuses on one problem, not a multi-feature platform.
2. **Short duration** — completed in 8-12 lab hours, not a full semester.
3. **Minimal external dependencies** — preferably uses Python Standard Library only.
4. **Demonstrable in lab** — must run and be explained within the viva slot.

*(Any four relevant points fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`
**Q: What is the "10-Concept Rule" for a KTU micro project? Name any six Python concepts that should ideally be demonstrated.**

**Model Answer:**

The 10-Concept Rule is an examiner heuristic stating that a good PCITL307 micro project should demonstrate at least 8-10 distinct Python programming concepts working together cohesively.

**Six essential concepts:**
1. **Data structures** — list, dictionary, set
2. **Control flow** — if/else, for/while loops
3. **Functions** — modular, reusable
4. **File handling** — `with open()`
5. **Exception handling** — try/except/finally
6. **Standard library modules** — `json`, `csv`, `os`, `datetime`

*(Any six from the concept matrix in Section 2.2 fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Micro Project Development Lifecycle (MPDL)** as expected by KTU for PCITL307. List and briefly describe **any five phases**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a micro project titled **"Personal Expense Tracker"** that allows a user to record daily expenses with **category, amount, date, and an optional note**, and persist them in a **CSV file**. Write the **complete Python code** (single file is acceptable) to implement the **"Add Expense"** and **"View All Expenses"** features. Ensure you use: a `while True` menu, `try/except` for input validation, `csv` module for file I/O, and `datetime` for date entry.

#### Model Solution

**(a) The MPDL has 6 phases:**

1. **Problem Identification & Scoping** — Choose a single, relatable, real-world problem. Define what is in-scope and out-of-scope to prevent scope creep.
2. **Requirement Analysis** — Enumerate functional requirements (user actions) and non-functional requirements (usability, response time). Decide on input/output formats and persistence mechanism.
3. **Design** — Sketch the high-level architecture (modules, functions, classes), data model (which data structures), and user interface flow (menu screens, GUI layout).
4. **Implementation** — Write modular, well-named, PEP 8-compliant code. Use functions or OOP. Add docstrings and comments.
5. **Testing & Debugging** — Test boundary cases (empty input, invalid input, duplicate entries, missing files), typical happy path, and edge cases. Fix bugs iteratively.
6. **Documentation & Presentation** — Write a README with project title, objective, features, technologies, how-to-run, and sample I/O. Prepare a 2-3 minute demo script and anticipate viva questions.

*(Any 5 phases with brief descriptions = 7 marks: ~1.4 marks each.)*

**(b) Sample Implementation:**

```python
"""
Personal Expense Tracker - Add and View features
Demonstrates: csv module, datetime, while loop, try/except,
              list of dicts, string formatting, functions.
"""

import csv
import os
from datetime import datetime

EXPENSE_FILE = "expenses.csv"


def load_expenses():
    """Load expenses from CSV; return [] if file missing."""
    if not os.path.exists(EXPENSE_FILE):
        return []
    expenses = []
    try:
        with open(EXPENSE_FILE, "r", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            reader = csv.DictReader(f)
            for row in reader:
                # Convert amount back to float
                row["amount"] = float(row["amount"])
                expenses.append(row)
    except (IOError, ValueError) as e:
        print(f"[ERROR] Could not read file: {e}")
    return expenses


def add_expense(expenses):
    """Add a new expense record."""
    print("\n--- ADD NEW EXPENSE ---")
    category = input("Enter category (Food/Travel/etc.): ").strip().title()
    if not category:
        print("[ERROR] Category cannot be empty.")
        return False
    try:
        amount = float(input("Enter amount (INR): "))
        if amount <= 0:
            print("[ERROR] Amount must be positive.")
            return False
    except ValueError:
        print("[ERROR] Invalid amount.")
        return False
    date_str = input("Enter date (YYYY-MM-DD) or press Enter for today: ").strip()
    if not date_str:
        date_str = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
    else:
        try:
            datetime.strptime(date_str, "%Y-%m-%d")
        except ValueError:
            print("[ERROR] Date must be in YYYY-MM-DD format.")
            return False
    note = input("Optional note: ").strip()

    record = {
        "date": date_str,
        "category": category,
        "amount": amount,
        "note": note,
    }
    expenses.append(record)

    # Persist to CSV (append mode)
    file_exists = os.path.exists(EXPENSE_FILE)
    try:
        with open(EXPENSE_FILE, "a", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            fieldnames = ["date", "category", "amount", "note"]
            writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=fieldnames)
            if not file_exists:
                writer.writeheader()
            writer.writerow(record)
        print(f"[SUCCESS] Expense of INR {amount} added.")
        return True
    except IOError as e:
        print(f"[ERROR] Could not write file: {e}")
        return False


def view_expenses(expenses):
    """Display all expenses in a formatted table."""
    if not expenses:
        print("\n[INFO] No expenses recorded yet.")
        return
    total = sum(e["amount"] for e in expenses)
    print("\n{:<12} {:<15} {:<10} {}".format(
        "Date", "Category", "Amount", "Note"
    ))
    print("-" * 60)
    for e in expenses:
        print("{:<12} {:<15} {:<10.2f} {}".format(
            e["date"], e["category"], e["amount"], e["note"]
        ))
    print("-" * 60)
    print(f"{'TOTAL':<12} {'':<15} {total:<10.2f}")


def main():
    expenses = load_expenses()
    print(f"[INFO] Loaded {len(expenses)} existing expense(s).")
    while True:
        print("\n1. Add Expense\n2. View All\n3. Exit")
        choice = input("Choice: ").strip()
        if choice == "1":
            add_expense(expenses)
        elif choice == "2":
            view_expenses(expenses)
        elif choice == "3":
            print("Goodbye!")
            break
        else:
            print("[ERROR] Invalid choice.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Mark Allocation Guidance for (b):**
- `csv` module usage (read/write) — **2 marks**
- `try/except` for validation — **1.5 marks**
- `while True` menu loop — **1 mark**
- `datetime` for date — **1 mark**
- Function modularity + `main()` guard — **1 mark**
- Code runs without error — **0.5 marks**

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the importance of **modular design** in a Python micro project. Illustrate with a diagram how a typical micro project can be split into **input, logic, storage, and output layers**, and explain the benefit of **separation of concerns**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the **Student Record Management System (SRMS)** described in Section 3.1, write the **complete Python code** for the `validators.py` module. Your code should include:
- A function to get a non-empty string
- A function to get a positive integer within a given range
- A function to validate a unique roll number against an existing list of records
- Proper use of `try/except`, `while` loops, and a clear docstring

#### Model Solution

**(a) Importance of Modular Design:**

Modular design means splitting a program into **multiple files/modules**, each handling one responsibility. Its importance:

1. **Readability** — Each file has a clear purpose; the code is easier to understand.
2. **Reusability** — Functions in one module can be imported and reused in another project.
3. **Easier debugging** — When a bug occurs, you know exactly which file to inspect.
4. **Parallel development** — In a team, different members can work on different modules simultaneously.
5. **Testability** — Each module can be unit-tested in isolation.

**Layered architecture diagram (refer to Section 4.5 Block Diagram):**

- **Input Layer** — collects user input (CLI prompts, menu choice).
- **Logic Layer** — performs validation, CRUD, search, sort.
- **Storage Layer** — persists and retrieves data (JSON/CSV/SQLite).
- **Output Layer** — formats and displays results.

**Separation of Concerns (SoC)** is the principle that *each module should address one concern*. Benefits: low coupling, high cohesion, easier maintenance, and cleaner viva explanations (you can point to one file per question).

*(7 marks: ~2 marks definition + importance, 3 marks diagram, 2 marks SoC explanation.)*

**(b) Sample Implementation for `validators.py`:**

*(Refer to the `validators.py` code in Section 3.1.4. The complete, working code is provided there with the following three functions: `get_non_empty_string`, `get_int_in_range`, `get_unique_roll`. The model answer matches that implementation exactly.)*

**Mark Allocation Guidance for (b):**
- Correct function signatures + docstrings — **1.5 marks**
- `try/except` for integer parsing — **2 marks**
- `while` loop for retry logic — **1.5 marks**
- Set-based uniqueness check for roll numbers — **1.5 marks**
- Code runs without error — **0.5 marks**

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Micro Project Evaluation:**
>
> 1. **No persistence layer** (5-mark penalty): A project that loses all data on exit will be heavily penalized. Always use file I/O or SQLite.
> 2. **Single monolithic file** (3-mark penalty): A 500-line `main.py` with no modularity suggests poor understanding. Split into at least 3-4 files.
> 3. **No exception handling** (2-3 mark penalty): Crashing on bad input is an instant red flag. Wrap risky operations in `try/except`.
> 4. **Missing README** (1-2 mark penalty): Examiners expect a 1-page README with objective, features, and how-to-run.
> 5. **Cannot explain their own code** (up to 5-mark penalty in viva): The most common cause of failure. If you cannot trace a function line-by-line, you will lose viva marks even if the code is correct.
> 6. **Using only basic concepts** (2-3 mark penalty): A project with *only* if-else and a list demonstrates shallow learning. Push for at least 8 concepts.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for PCITL307 Micro Project:**

- A **micro project** is a small, end-to-end Python application (CLI, GUI, or data-processing) built by 1-4 students in 8-12 lab hours.
- The **MPDL** has 6 phases: Problem ID → Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Documentation.
- The **10-Concept Rule**: Your project should demonstrate at least **8-10 distinct Python concepts** working together.
- **Mandatory concepts** to include: data structures, control flow, functions, file I/O, exception handling, standard library modules, and (ideally) one OOP class.
- **File structure**: Split into at least `main.py`, a logic file, a storage file, and a validators file. Use a `README.md`.
- **Persistence**: Use **JSON** (simple), **CSV** (tabular), or **SQLite3** (relational). Never store data only in memory.
- **Exception handling**: Wrap *every* user-input read and *every* file operation in `try/except`.
- **Validation**: Always validate: non-empty strings, integer ranges, unique IDs.
- **Idioms to use**: `with open()`, `if __name__ == "__main__":`, f-strings, list/dict comprehensions, `enumerate`, `lambda` with `filter`/`map`/`sorted`.
- **Viva prep**: Be ready to explain *every line*, justify *every data structure choice*, and discuss *time complexity* of your operations.
- **Common project ideas**: Student Record System, Expense Tracker, Quiz App, Library Management, To-Do List, Contact Book, Inventory System, Timetable Generator, Simple Chatbot, Weather Logger.
- **Anti-patterns to avoid**: Single-file projects, no persistence, no validation, hardcoded paths, no comments, poor naming, unused imports.
